text: The laparoscopic procedure begins with the surgeon taking down the gastrocolic ligament. The right gastroepiploic artery is identified and taken down, followed by the right gastroepiploic vein. The gastrocolic trunk is clipped, and the gastrocolic vein is divided. The SMV is visualized, and a partial tunnel is created behind the neck of the pancreas.

The surgeon performs a Kocher maneuver, retracting the second part of the duodenum to expose the inferior vena cava (IVC). The third part of the duodenum is also mobilized, completing the extended Kocherization. The duodenojejunal (DJ) flexure is then fully mobilized, and the jejunum is divided 15 centimeters from the DJ flexure using a 60 mm stapler. The mesentery is dissected up to the DJ flexure and repositioned into the supracolic compartment. The duodenum is reflected cranially, revealing the inferior pancreaticoduodenal vessels branching from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and vein (SMV). The inferior pancreaticoduodenal vein is clipped and cut, allowing visualization of the SMA.

The surgeon divides the inferior pancreaticoduodenal veins, reflecting the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) to reveal the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). The inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery is identified arising from the SMA, and additional pancreaticoduodenal vessels are clipped and divided to minimize bleeding. The falciform ligament is lifted using transabdominal sutures. The stomach is transected proximal to the pylorus using a golden 60 mm stapler, following the clipping of the right gastroepiploic artery and vein, and the right gastric artery is taken down from the hepatic artery. The lesser sac is opened and released to facilitate anastomosis.

The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy on the lymph nodes located on the common hepatic artery, rolling down the superior border of the pancreas with the left hand. A vessel sealing device is used to expose the common hepatic artery, which gives rise to the proper hepatic artery and the gastroduodenal artery. The gastroduodenal artery is dissected, clamped, double clipped, and divided, leaving a stump. The portal vein is exposed after the division of the gastroduodenal artery, revealing the splenic vein branching into it. The neck of the pancreas is looped with umbilical tape and tied proximally. The uncinate process is separated from the SMA, and the neck of the pancreas is transected using monopolar cautery, with potential bleeding at the upper and lower poles.

The surgeon exposes and sharply divides the duct of the uncinate process without using an energy source, followed by the division of the cephalic part of the pancreas body. Bleeding points are controlled, and the common bile duct (CBD) is dissected from the portal vein. The cystic duct and artery are dissected, with the cystic artery taken down. The right hepatic artery is seen moving anterior to the CBD, aiding in traction for exposure. The cystic duct is divided due to its high entry into the CBD. After lymphadenectomy, the CBD is divided distal to the right hepatic artery for anastomosis. The surgical field reveals the portal vein, splenic vein, superior mesenteric vein (SMV), and the SMA region. The jejunum is brought through the mesocolic window for pancreaticojejunostomy using the modified Blunger technique.

The surgeon performs ductomucosa sutures on the atrophic pancreas using 4-0 PDS interrupted sutures. Through-and-through sutures are placed cephalad and caudal to the duct with 2-0 Prolene, ensuring snug fit of the seromuscular layer of the jejunum into the posterior part of the pancreas. Five sutures are taken on the posterior wall and five on the anterior wall, with careful placement to avoid gaps. The final sutures are tied under vision to complete the ductomucosa anastomosis.

The surgeon takes a proline suture through the anterior wall of the jejunum, rolling it over the anterior part of the pancreas, similar to the posterior placement. Capsular to seromuscular bites are taken on the anterior wall of the jejunum at the duct site, ensuring a snug fit. Another through-and-through bite is taken caudally, covering the transected surface of the pancreas with the jejunum, completing the modified Blunger technique pancreaticojejunostomy. The hepaticojejunostomy begins with the epigastric port retracting the left liver lobe. An intratomy is performed, and continuous V-lock sutures are placed between the wide duct and jejunum, ensuring full thickness coverage. The posterior layer of sutures is completed, followed by the anterior layer, with careful retraction to avoid the posterior wall. After securing the sutures, a clip is applied for safety, and bile leak is checked, confirming absence.

The gallbladder is removed, and surgical drains are placed near the hepatico-jejunostomy and pancreatic jejunostomy sites. A loop of jejunum is selected and tied to the stomach, and a small incision is made at the umbilicus for gastrojejunostomy (GJ) and feeding jejunostomy (FJ).
